summary:
The Battle of Tsushima Strait on 27–28 May 1905 was the decisive naval engagement of the [[event:2561|Russo-Japanese War]] and one of the most complete naval victories in modern history. Admiral Togo Heihachiro's Japanese Combined Fleet annihilated the Russian Baltic Squadron under Admiral Rozhestvensky after it had sailed over 30,000 kilometers from the Baltic to Asian waters in a desperate attempt to relieve the besieged Russian Pacific Fleet at Port Arthur.

The Russian Baltic Fleet's voyage was an odyssey of mishaps and delays. It had sailed in October 1904, more than seven months before the battle, and had been denied coaling rights in most ports by the international law of neutrality. In the North Sea it had infamously fired on British fishing trawlers, mistaking them for Japanese torpedo boats, nearly causing a war with Britain. The fleet arrived in East Asian waters in poor condition with worn crews and fouled hulls.

Admiral Togo, forewarned of the fleet's approach, positioned his force in the Tsushima Strait between Japan and Korea. The Japanese 'crossing the T' maneuver and superior gunnery proved devastating. In two days of fighting, the Japanese sank 21 of the 38 Russian warships, captured 7 more, and killed over 4,000 Russian sailors; Japanese losses were minimal — 3 torpedo boats sunk and about 600 casualties. Rozhestvensky himself was wounded and captured.

Tsushima shocked the world. It confirmed Japan's status as a major naval power and demonstrated that an Asian nation could employ the most sophisticated modern naval tactics with decisive effect against a European navy. The battle's outcome contributed directly to the Russian Revolution of 1905 by adding a spectacular military humiliation to the social and political grievances already straining tsarist authority.
